steps.title: Welcome to Day 18: AI for Product Development
steps.body:
Product development is the art of turning ideas into reality — but the research, specification, and prioritization that come before building can take weeks. AI compresses that timeline dramatically.

Today you'll learn how to:
• Rapidly prototype ideas using AI as a thinking partner
• Generate comprehensive PRDs (Product Requirements Documents) in minutes
• Automate user research — analysis, synthesis, and insights extraction
• Apply feature prioritization frameworks that use data, not gut feelings

By the end of today, you'll be able to go from concept to spec in hours instead of weeks.

AI at every stage of product development:

💡 IDEATION — AI analyzes market gaps, user feedback, competitor features, and technology trends to generate product ideas worth exploring.

📋 SPECIFICATION — AI generates structured PRDs: problem statement, user stories, acceptance criteria, technical considerations, edge cases.

🔬 USER RESEARCH — AI analyzes interview transcripts, survey responses, and usage data to surface patterns, pain points, and opportunities.

⚖️ PRIORITIZATION — AI applies frameworks (RICE, MoSCoW, Kano Model) to score and rank features objectively.

🧪 PROTOTYPING — AI generates wireframes, user flows, UI copy, and even code snippets for rapid testing.

AI doesn't decide what to build — it makes sure the decision is informed by data, not opinion.

steps.question: What's the biggest risk of using AI to generate PRDs and product specifications?
options: AI specs are too detailed and hard to read
AI might suggest features that look reasonable but aren't grounded in real user needs — you miss the 'why' behind the spec
AI-generated specs take as long as writing them manually
feedback: Exactly. AI can generate a beautiful spec that looks complete but is missing the essential ingredient: deep understanding of user pain. Always start with real user research and problem validation before using AI to write the spec. The AI formalizes your understanding; it doesn't replace it.

Not quite. AI can and should handle technical specs, but the risk is generating features that seem logical without addressing real user needs. The 'why' must come from real human research. Never let AI's plausible-sounding outputs replace genuine user understanding.

steps.question: AI-generated user research analysis is usually more objective than human analysis, because AI doesn't have unconscious biases.
feedback: Correct. AI has its own biases — training data bias, recency bias, and the tendency to produce the 'most likely' answer rather than the 'most true' one. AI can help surface patterns in large datasets, but human interpretation of user research is essential for depth and context.

Not true. AI has training biases, recency biases, and a tendency toward safe, conventional answers. It's excellent at summarizing large volumes of interview transcripts and flagging recurring themes, but it needs human context to interpret what those themes actually mean for users.

steps.title: How Nina's AI Assistant Cut Spec Time by 80%
steps.body:
Nina is a product manager at a fintech startup. Her pain point: writing PRDs for new features took 2-3 days of research, drafting, and refining with engineering.

Her AI workflow:

1. USER RESEARCH (Day 1): Nina uploads 15 customer interview transcripts. AI identifies top 5 pain points, ranks them by frequency, and extracts verbatim quotes for each. Thematic analysis that would take 8 hours takes 20 minutes.

2. PROBLEM DEFINITION (Day 1): AI synthesizes findings into a one-page problem statement with supporting evidence. Nina reviews and adjusts.

3. PRD GENERATION (Day 1-2): Nina gives AI the problem statement + 3 example features she's considering. AI generates full PRDs including:
   • User stories with acceptance criteria
   • Edge cases and error states
   • Technical considerations
   • Success metrics
   • Dependencies and risks

4. PRIORITIZATION (Day 2): AI scores each feature using RICE framework (Reach, Impact, Confidence, Effort). Team reviews the ranked list and picks the sprint.

5. ITERATION (Day 2): Engineering reviews and asks questions. AI updates PRDs based on technical feedback in minutes, not days.

RESULT: Nina's 3-day PRD process became an afternoon. Quality improved because AI never forgets edge cases or acceptance criteria. The team ships faster because specs are complete and clear.

steps.question: The product prioritization framework that scores features based on Reach, Impact, Confidence, and Effort is called the ___ framework.
feedback: Correct! RICE (Reach, Impact, Confidence, Effort) is one of the most popular objective prioritization frameworks. It forces teams to define and score each dimension rather than relying on 'my feature is more important' debates.

The term is RICE — Reach, Impact, Confidence, Effort. It's a structured way to compare features on the same dimensions. AI is great at scoring RICE because it can estimate reach from data and effort from past work, but the team validates the scores before deciding.

steps.title: AI-Generated User Story vs. Manual
steps.body:
BEFORE — A typical user story written by a busy PM:
"As a user, I want to reset my password so I can log in."

That's it. No acceptance criteria. No edge cases. Engineering has to ask 10 questions before they can build it.

AFTER — AI-generated from a simple prompt:
"Generate a complete user story and acceptance criteria for password reset functionality, including email flow, security considerations, and error states."

AS A USER, I want to reset my password
SO THAT I can regain access to my account

ACCEPTANCE CRITERIA:
• User can request a password reset from the login page
• System sends a reset email with a unique, time-limited link (expires in 15 minutes)
• Link opens a secure reset page (HTTPS, token validation)
• User enters new password with strength indicator (min 8 chars, special char required)
• Confirmation of reset, redirect to login

EDGE CASES:
• Email not found → Show generic 'if account exists, you'll receive an email' message
• Expired link → Show clear error with 'resend' option
• Multiple reset requests → Only last token is valid
• Account locked → Reset password also unlocks account
• API failure → Log error, notify user to try again, alert engineering

Which spec would you rather build from? The AI-generated one gives engineering everything they need. The PM still owns the problem and decisions — AI just handles the thoroughness.

steps.question: Using AI to analyze 200 customer support tickets and identify the top 5 feature requests with supporting evidence (frequency, sentiment, revenue impact).
feedback: This is an ideal use case. AI processes unstructured text at scale — categorizing requests, measuring sentiment, linking tickets to features. A human would take days to do this manually and might miss patterns. AI surfaces the signal; the product team validates and prioritizes.

Excellent fit. High-volume unstructured analysis is where AI excels. 200 tickets would take a PM an entire day to read and categorize. AI does it in minutes and often finds patterns humans miss — like related requests that appear across different categories.

steps.question: Making the final decision on which feature to build next, balancing strategic goals, user needs, and business constraints.
feedback: Exactly right. AI provides the data — RICE scores, user feedback analysis, market research — but the final prioritization decision is a product leader's judgment call. Strategic alignment, organizational politics, timing, and vision are human considerations that no algorithm captures fully.

Correct — the final decision is human work. Your job is to gather AI-synthesized data, discuss with the team, and decide. AI informs; you decide. The best product teams use AI to ensure their decisions are data-informed, not data-driven without context.

fields.label: What user problem are you solving?
fields.placeholder: e.g., Users can't easily find products they previously viewed

fields.label: Who is this for?
fields.placeholder: e.g., Returning shoppers who browse without purchasing

fields.label: How will you measure success?
fields.placeholder: e.g., 20% increase in repeat visits, 15% higher conversion rate

fields.label: What are your constraints?
fields.placeholder: e.g., Must work on mobile, must be built in 2 sprints, must not slow page load

steps.body:
PRODUCT AI PROMPT TEMPLATE

"You are a senior product manager with deep experience in [INDUSTRY].

I need a PRD for a feature that solves this problem:
[DESCRIBE PROBLEM]

Target users: [DESCRIBE]
Business context: [DESCRIBE]
Constraints: [DESCRIBE]

Generate a PRD with these sections:

1. EXECUTIVE SUMMARY — Problem, proposed solution, expected impact (3 sentences)
2. USER STORIES — 3-5 stories with full acceptance criteria each
3. EDGE CASES — What could go wrong? List 5-10 edge cases with expected behavior
4. TECHNICAL NOTES — Architecture impact, dependencies, performance considerations
5. SUCCESS METRICS — How we'll know it worked (leading + lagging indicators)
6. OPEN QUESTIONS — What we still need to decide

Be specific. Avoid generic PM language. Write this so an engineer could start building from it."

Think of a feature or product you'd like to build — even a simple one. Use the PRD prompt template to generate a spec with an AI tool. Paste the output here and note: what edge cases or details did the AI catch that you wouldn't have thought of?

steps.question: A feature has high Reach and Impact but also very high Effort. According to RICE prioritization, what should you do?
options: Build it immediately — reach and impact are the only things that matter
Score it properly — divide (Reach × Impact × Confidence) by Effort — and compare against other features. A high-effort feature might still be worth it if the ratio is good
Skip it entirely — high effort features are never worth it
Reduce effort by building a half version without the hard parts
feedback: Right. RICE divides (Reach × Impact × Confidence) by Effort to get a per-unit-effort score. A high-effort feature with massive impact might still be the right choice — but it might also lose to a medium-impact feature that takes a fraction of the effort. Score everything, then compare.

Not quite. You need to compute the full RICE score and compare. A feature with Reach=8, Impact=8, Confidence=8 and Effort=8 scores 8.0. A smaller feature with Reach=4, Impact=4, Confidence=9, Effort=2 scores 7.2 — almost as high for much less effort. The ratio is what matters.

steps.prompt:
Think about a product or feature you'd love to build.

• What user problem does it solve?
• What data do you have (or need) to validate it?
• What would an AI-generated PRD look like for this feature?
• Which part of the product development process would AI help you most with?

questions.label: What part of product development do you find most challenging?
options: Ideation — coming up with good ideas worth building
User research — gathering and synthesizing feedback
Prioritization — deciding what to build first

questions.label: What feature or product would you spec with AI first?
questions.placeholder: Describe the idea and how you'd use AI in each stage...

steps.title: Day 18 Complete! 💡
steps.body:
You now know how AI accelerates every stage of product development — from ideation and specification to user research, prioritization, and prototyping.

Key takeaways:
• AI generates thorough PRDs with edge cases humans often miss
• User research analysis is 10x faster with AI — but still needs human interpretation
• RICE scoring removes 'loudest voice wins' from prioritization
• AI specs are starting points, not final documents — validate with your team
• The best product teams use AI to be data-informed, not automated

Tomorrow: AI for Finance & HR — financial analysis, budgeting, recruitment, and employee onboarding automation.
